case_studies.title: Metabolic Risk in a Child with Dysglycemia and Dyslipidemia
case_studies.vignette: A 12-year-old girl from a semi-urban area in India presents to the outpatient clinic with complaints of fatigue and darkened skin patches on her neck and axillae for the past six months. Her mother reports a recent increase in weight despite normal eating habits and no significant physical activity. The girl has no history of fever, polyuria, or polydipsia. On examination, her BMI is above the 95th percentile for age, and there is prominent acanthosis nigricans on the neck and axillae. Blood pressure is 122/78 mmHg (elevated for age and height). Initial lab results show fasting glucose of 102 mg/dL, HbA1c of 6.1%, and fasting triglycerides of 280 mg/dL, with HDL-C at 38 mg/dL. There is no family history of diabetes, but her father has hyperlipidemia. The family has limited access to healthy food options, relying heavily on processed snacks and sugary beverages.
case_studies.learning_objective: To evaluate and interpret dysglycemia and dyslipidemia in children, identify discordant glycemic markers, and make decisions on triage, lifestyle interventions, and follow-up testing.

case_studies.title: Dysglycemia and Dyslipidemia in a 12-Year-Old Boy
case_studies.vignette: A 12-year-old boy presents to a rural clinic in India with complaints of fatigue, increased thirst, and occasional headaches over the past 3 months. His mother reports a significant weight gain of 6 kg over the last year and a strong family history of type 2 diabetes mellitus in both parents. The boy’s diet consists largely of fried snacks, sugary beverages, and minimal physical activity. On examination, his BMI is at the 98th percentile for age, and he has acanthosis nigricans over his neck and axillae. Blood pressure is 128/84 mmHg (above the 95th percentile). Initial laboratory testing reveals fasting glucose of 102 mg/dL, HbA1c of 6.1%, and fasting triglycerides of 300 mg/dL with low HDL-C (38 mg/dL). The child is asymptomatic otherwise, with no history of fever or acute illness.
case_studies.learning_objective: To evaluate metabolic risk in children presenting with dysglycemia and dyslipidemia, interpret discordant glycemic markers, and determine triage thresholds for further testing or intervention.

subtopics.subtopic_title: MASLD HYPERTENSION AND GENETIC DYSLIPIDEMIA CASE DECISION PATHWAYS
subtopics.category: CASE-BASED DECISION PRACTICE IN METABOLIC RISK CLINICS
subtopics.concept: The management of metabolic-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD), hypertension, and genetic dyslipidemia in paediatric patients requires case-specific decision pathways to address individual risks and comorbidities effectively. In the context of MASLD, a 14-year-old boy with class 2 obesity and an ALT level of 90 IU/L, despite a “normal” ultrasound, should be treated as probable MASLD. Ultrasound can miss hepatic steatosis, and ALT levels exceeding twice the sex-specific upper limit are clinically significant regardless of imaging findings. The recommended approach includes initiating structured lifestyle therapy, rechecking ALT in approximately 12 weeks, and planning elastography or hepatology referral if ALT levels persist. For persistent stage 1 hypertension, as seen in a 12-year-old with obesity and auscultatory blood pressure consistently in the stage 1 range across three visits, confirmation and further evaluation are essential. Ambulatory blood pressure monitoring (ABPM) is recommended to differentiate white-coat or masked hypertension and assess target-organ risk. Management should include screening for metabolic comorbidities such as glycemia, lipids, and ALT, intensifying weight-focused interventions, and considering echocardiography if pharmacotherapy is being contemplated. In cases of suspected familial hypercholesterolemia, such as a 9-year-old with normal weight and fasting LDL-C of 205 mg/dL but unclear family history, LDL-C levels ≥190 mg/dL strongly suggest familial hypercholesterolemia even in the absence of a documented family history. The management pathway involves repeating fasting lipid profiles for confirmation, excluding secondary causes, initiating an LDL-targeted diet, and referring the patient to a lipid specialist for further management, including cascade screening of parents. Lastly, in adolescents with obesity driven by reproductive and mental health factors, such as a 16-year-old girl with class 2 obesity, irregular menses, acne, and binge eating, screening for polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) risk and dysglycemia is critical. Mental health should be addressed as a metabolic modifier, necessitating evaluation for androgen excess, metabolic labs, depression/anxiety screening, and collaboration with adolescent gynecology, endocrinology, and mental health specialists. Restrictive diets should be avoided to prevent binge-restrict cycles. These case-based pathways emphasize the importance of tailored, multidisciplinary approaches in paediatric metabolic risk management.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is 'Repeat fasting lipid profile.' Confirming LDL-C elevation ≥190 mg/dL and excluding secondary causes are key steps in managing suspected familial hypercholesterolemia.

choices.rationale: Parent screening occurs after confirming FH diagnosis in the child.
Repeating lipid profile confirms LDL-C elevation and excludes secondary causes.
Statins are not initiated without confirmation of FH diagnosis.
Genetic testing is not the first-line diagnostic step for FH.

questions.stem: What is the next step in managing this child’s elevated ALT and LDL-C?
questions.explanation: Lifestyle modifications, including dietary changes and increased physical activity, are the first-line management for obesity-associated dyslipidemia and elevated ALT.

choices.rationale: Repeat ALT in 12 weeks is appropriate but secondary to initiating lifestyle changes first.
Lifestyle modifications are the cornerstone of managing obesity-associated dyslipidemia and elevated ALT.
Endocrinology referral is unnecessary unless further metabolic or endocrine abnormalities are identified.
Lipid-lowering medication is premature without further evaluation and structured lifestyle interventions.

questions.stem: A 14-year-old boy with class 2 obesity has an ALT level of 90 IU/L and a normal ultrasound. What is the next best step in management?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is 'Start lifestyle therapy, recheck ALT.' Structured lifestyle therapy and monitoring ALT in 12 weeks are essential for managing probable MASLD despite normal imaging.

choices.rationale: Referral is premature unless ALT persists after initial management.
Lifestyle therapy and ALT monitoring are recommended for probable MASLD.
Biopsy is invasive and not first-line for probable MASLD diagnosis.
Ultrasound often misses steatosis; repeating it is not the best option.

choices.rationale: Medication is not first-line for stage 1 hypertension without further evaluation.

case_studies.title: Metabolic Risk Assessment in a 12-Year-Old with Obesity and Hypertension
case_studies.vignette: A 12-year-old boy presents to a paediatric outpatient clinic in an urban hospital in India with concerns of elevated blood pressure noted during a school health check-up. His parents report a history of poor dietary habits, minimal physical activity, and recent weight gain over the past year. There is no significant family history of hypertension or cardiovascular disease. On examination, his BMI is above the 95th percentile for age and sex. Auscultatory blood pressure measured in the right arm is 132/84 mmHg, consistent with stage 1 hypertension, confirmed on three separate visits over the past three months. The rest of the systemic examination is unremarkable. Basic investigations reveal a fasting LDL-C of 150 mg/dL and ALT of 60 IU/L. Fasting glucose and renal function tests are normal. The family is concerned about the implications of his blood pressure and cholesterol levels. The paediatrician faces a decision on further evaluation and management steps, including the need for ambulatory blood pressure monitoring, lifestyle interventions, and specialist referrals.
case_studies.learning_objective: Understand the decision-making process for evaluating and managing stage 1 hypertension and metabolic risks, including dyslipidemia and MASLD, in a paediatric patient with obesity.

mcqs.stem: What is the next best step for evaluating this 12-year-old boy with stage 1 hypertension and elevated LDL-C?
mcqs.explanation: Ambulatory blood pressure monitoring (ABPM) is essential to confirm persistent hypertension, differentiate white-coat or masked hypertension, and assess potential target-organ damage.

choices.rationale: Hepatology referral is not indicated unless ALT levels worsen or MASLD is strongly suspected.
Antihypertensive medication is premature without further evaluation of persistent hypertension.
Echocardiography is reserved for assessing target-organ damage if pharmacotherapy is planned.
Ambulatory BP monitoring confirms persistent hypertension and rules out white-coat effect.

subtopics.subtopic_title: ONE- PAGE INTAKE AND FOLLOW-UP TEMPLATE FIELDS
subtopics.category: CLINIC TEMPLATES AND EXAM CUTPOINT  RECAP
subtopics.concept: A standardized one-page intake and follow-up template is essential for identifying and managing metabolic risk in paediatric patients, as it minimizes missed comorbidities and ensures consistent follow-up across clinicians. The intake sheet should comprehensively record height, weight, BMI category, and waist circumference, along with blood pressure (BP) readings, ensuring cuff size and repeat measurements are documented. Pubertal stage and signs of acanthosis nigricans should be noted, alongside a symptom screen for polyuria, polydipsia, nocturia, snoring, apnea, headaches, abdominal pain, and menstrual irregularities. A detailed medication list must highlight any obesogenic drugs, while family history should include premature atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD) and familial hypercholesterolemia. A “tiered lab plan” should specify which tests are ordered immediately and which are deferred due to cost considerations. The follow-up sheet should track outcomes beyond weight, including waist circumference trends, BMI slope, sleep schedule goals, sugary beverage reduction to zero, and daily physical activity targets. Abnormal baseline laboratory results should be listed, with scheduled recheck dates based on the patient’s risk stage, such as ALT or fasting lipid levels at 12-week intervals. A referral box should be included to avoid delays in consulting hepatology, endocrinology, lipid clinics, or sleep medicine when indicated. For quick reference during viva exams or clinical practice, a concise “cutpoint recap” section is recommended. This should include India-specific obesity cutoffs (overweight: BMI 23; obesity: BMI 27; class 2 and 3 obesity: 120–140% and ≥140% of BMI 27, respectively), lipid thresholds (high LDL-C ≥130, high non-HDL-C ≥145, high triglycerides ≥100 in ages 0–9 and ≥130 in ages 10–19), prediabetes and diabetes thresholds (fasting glucose 100–125 and ≥126 mg/dL; HbA1c 5.7–6.4% and ≥6.5%), and metabolic-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD) significance defined as ALT >2× the sex-specific upper limit. These structured templates ensure a systematic approach to paediatric metabolic risk assessment and follow-up.
